skill.name: code-analysis
skill.description: Check if code is readable by non-developers - clear names, plain English comments, no jargon
skill.summary: Check if code is readable by non-developers - clear names, plain English comments, no jargon
skill.author: abereyes

security_audit.summary: The static weak cryptography findings are false positives caused by readability terms such as token, serialize, and hash-related wording, not cryptographic operations. The Markdown backtick findings are also false positives from fenced examples and documented CLI usage. The skill is safe to publish with a low warning because it runs a local script that reads user-selected files and may print code snippets.

low_findings.title: Local File Read Requires User Care
low_findings.description: The analyzer accepts a user-provided --path value, checks that the path exists, and reads the file contents for analysis. This is expected for a readability tool, but users should avoid scanning files that contain secrets or sensitive source because snippets can be included in output.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly reads local files from a user-supplied path and can report snippets. No evidence found of network exfiltration, shell execution, or hidden persistence.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged locations use words such as token, serialize, JSON, and descriptions for readability issues. No evidence found of weak hashing, encryption, or cryptographic APIs in the scanned files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are static text, comments, labels, or readability suggestions. They do not call MD5, SHA-1, encryption routines, random generators, or password handling logic.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The SKILL.md findings are Markdown fenced examples and documented usage for running the local analyzer. They are not Ruby shell backticks and do not create hidden command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The backticks delimit Markdown code blocks. The only command shown is an explicit user-facing python3 invocation for the local script.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Static Finding Is False Positive
low_findings.description: The line flagged for system reconnaissance resets an internal counter after reporting a readability issue. No evidence found of host enumeration, system probing, or environment collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a local state reset inside comment-ratio analysis. It has no operating system calls, network calls, or data collection behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Selected Local File Reading
dangerous_patterns.description: The script reads a local file path supplied by the user to perform readability checks. This is necessary behavior, but it should be limited to intended project files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file access is explicit and local. The reviewed code does not show network transfer or command execution around the read operation.

content.value_statement: Teams often write code that only developers can follow. This skill checks names, comments, jargon, and documentation so broader teams can understand code faster.
actual_capabilities: Scores readability for supported source files.
Flags cryptic variable and function names.
Checks comment coverage and long uncommented sections.
Finds unexplained technical jargon in comments.
Checks functions and classes for What, Why, and How documentation.
limitations: Only supports Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, Go, Ruby, and PHP extensions.
It reads local files selected by the user.
It can produce false positives in examples or generated code.
It measures readability patterns, not code correctness or security.

use_cases.title: Review stakeholder-facing code
use_cases.description: Check whether business logic has clear names and plain comments before a product review.

use_cases.title: Prepare onboarding examples
use_cases.description: Find missing explanations before sharing code with new team members.

use_cases.title: Improve documentation quality
use_cases.description: Identify jargon and missing What, Why, and How context in source comments.

prompt_templates.title: Check one file
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the code-analysis skill to review this file for non-developer readability: [file path].

prompt_templates.title: Focus on naming
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze [file path] with focus on naming. Explain which names should be clearer and why.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare for onboarding
prompt_templates.prompt: Review [file path] for a new team member. Prioritize comments, jargon, and missing context.

prompt_templates.title: Run strict readiness review
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze [file path] with strict readability expectations. Summarize blockers before this code is shared with stakeholders.

output_examples.input: A Python file with short variable names and few comments.
output_examples.output: The report gives a moderate score, flags unclear names, and suggests fuller names with explanatory comments.

output_examples.input: A TypeScript file with API jargon in comments.
output_examples.output: The report identifies unexplained technical terms and suggests plain English definitions.
The report gives a high score and notes that the file is accessible to non-developers.

best_practices: Run it on intended project files, not files that contain secrets.
Use strict mode before sharing code with non-developer audiences.
Review each suggestion manually before changing established domain terms.
anti_patterns: Do not treat the score as proof that the code is correct.
Do not scan private credentials, keys, or sensitive configuration files.
Do not replace human documentation review with automated findings alone.

faq.question: What does this skill analyze?
faq.answer: It analyzes readability signals such as naming, comments, jargon, and section documentation.

faq.question: Does it fix code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It reports issues and suggestions so a user can decide what to change.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The marketplace metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Can it review a whole repository?
faq.answer: The included script is designed around a path input, but the documented example shows single-file usage.

faq.question: Is this a security scanner?
faq.answer: No. It checks readability and documentation quality, not vulnerabilities.

faq.question: What should users avoid?
faq.answer: Users should avoid scanning files with secrets because output can include code snippets.
